run.output: Query executed successfully, produced output to Q3.csv

validation.comparison_result: Q3 results match ground truth: 10 rows expected, 10 rows produced. Rows correct (verified via compare_results.py)

Q3.notes: Results correct. Execution time 429ms is slightly slower than baseline (370ms avg per benchmark). Top revenue order: 4791171 with revenue 440715.22.

Q3.bottleneck: Hash table and aggregation performance in lineitem scan phase. Sub-optimal hash function quality and data structure choice for 64-thread merge.
Q3.root_cause: std::unordered_map uses linked-list chaining with poor cache locality; GroupKey hash function uses low-quality XOR-based hashing; Bloom filter false positive rate ~10% causes unnecessary hash table lookups; thread-local aggregation merge phase sequentially probes 64 separate hash tables.

analysis.overall: Query is functionally correct and executes in 429ms, slightly above baseline of 370ms. Main opportunity: improve hash table performance (2-5x possible) and reduce merge-phase overhead. Comparison to DuckDB (99ms) shows 4.3x gap, primarily due to hash table quality and SIMD opportunities not yet explored.

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace std::unordered_map with compact hash table (Robin Hood or Swiss Tables)
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses std::unordered_map<GroupKey, AggregateState> for aggregation (line 430) and std::unordered_map<int32_t, std::pair<...>> for order lookup (line 342). These use linked-list chaining causing: (1) poor cache locality during probe (average probe chain length >1 with 64-thread contention), (2) malloc fragmentation across 114K+ allocations. Replace with open-addressing hash table using Robin Hood hashing or Abseil SwissTable for: (a) single contiguous array (cache-friendly), (b) short probe chains (DIB-based stealing), (c) minimal allocations. Expected: 2-3x faster hash lookups in lineitem scan loop (most time spent here).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 35-50% total query time reduction (from 429ms to 250-280ms). Hash table lookups dominate lineitem loop; open-addressing reduces probe chain length and memory latency.

performance_optimizations.technique: Improve GroupKey hash function with XXH3 or MurmurHash
performance_optimizations.description: Current hash function (lines 47-55) uses XOR-based hashing: h1 ^ (h2 << 1) ^ (h3 << 2). This is low-quality; collisions cause probe chain length expansion. Replace with single-pass high-quality hash (XXH3 or MurmurHash). Combine 3 int32_t fields into single hash value using bit-mixing. Can reduce collision rate from ~5-8% to <1% for typical TPC-H data distribution.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% faster hash table operations (shorter probe chains, fewer redundant lookups). Total: ~20-30ms savings on 429ms base.

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimize Bloom filter sizing and hash function quality
performance_optimizations.description: Current Bloom filter: 4M bits for 1.46M order keys = 1.3 bits/key, 2 hash functions → false positive rate ~10%. Every non-matching lineitem triggers Bloom miss, but many pass Bloom check and fail hash table lookup. Options: (1) increase bits to 6-8M (8M bits = 10.9 KB, still fits L1 cache), reduce FP rate to 1-2%; (2) use better hash function (xxhash-based instead of simple mixing on lines 101-110). Expected FP reduction: 10% → 2% false positives, reducing unnecessary hash lookups by ~8% of lineitem rows.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% improvement on lineitem scan loop (reduced spurious hash lookups). Total: 20-40ms savings.

performance_optimizations.technique: Partition lineitem results before merging thread-local aggregations
performance_optimizations.description: Current merge (lines 430-435): single global map, 64 threads each insert entries into it with atomic contention (implicit on std::unordered_map reallocation). Instead: (1) partition 114K groups by hash(group_key) % 64; (2) each thread responsible for own partition; (3) threads write to disjoint partitions (no contention); (4) global map built from partition results. Reduces coordination overhead and improves cache locality during merge phase.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% improvement on merge phase (currently O(n) with contention). Total: 15-30ms savings.

performance_optimizations.technique: Prefetch and SIMD-vectorize Bloom filter + shipdate filter combination
performance_optimizations.description: Hot loop (lines 389-416): for each of 60M lineitem rows, load 4 values (l_orderkey, l_shipdate, l_extendedprice, l_discount) and filter. Currently scalar with dependent loads (shipdate check before Bloom check). Opportunity: (1) batch-prefetch l_orderkey values ahead (8-16 rows), (2) SIMD-evaluate shipdate > DATE_1995_03_15 for 8 rows in parallel using AVX2, (3) then sequential Bloom checks (Bloom cannot be easily vectorized). Reduces instruction latency and improves IPC.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-15% on lineitem scanning (large data, scalar bottleneck). Total: 30-50ms savings.

summary: Q3 iteration 1 is functionally correct (results match ground truth) and executes in 429ms. Primary bottleneck is sub-optimal hash table implementation (std::unordered_map) and merge-phase overhead. Recommended quick wins: (1) replace with compact hash table (Robin Hood/Swiss Tables) for 2-3x speedup on lookups, (2) improve hash function quality, (3) optimize Bloom filter. These changes should close ~50% of the gap to DuckDB (429ms → 280ms target). Further SIMD vectorization of filtering could provide additional 10-15% improvement. All recommended optimizations have low-medium risk and are well-established techniques.
